preamble: The transformation of the voiced postalveolar fricative /ʒ/ to the voiceless alveolar fricative /s/ is a phonological shift characterized by both devoicing and fronting. This change involves the vocal cords ceasing vibration and the point of articulation moving forward in the mouth, from the postalveolar region to the alveolar ridge. This shift is a natural phonetic process, often observed in language development and historical sound changes.

examples.note: This shift is observed in early French loanwords borrowed into Afrikaans, indicating a regular historical sound change.
The devoicing of postalveolar fricatives, including /ʒ/ to /s/, is a documented characteristic of the Welsh English dialect.

Depalatalization of etymological postalveolar fricatives, including /ʒ/, to /s/ is a characteristic feature of Guinea-Bissau Creole. While the source provides examples for /ʃ/ to /s/, it explicitly states the rule applies to both /ʃ/ and /ʒ/.

sources: van der Merwe, M. J. (2014). From Cape Dutch to Afrikaans: A Comparison of Phonemic Inventories. Utrecht University.
Jones, B. A. (2016). A History of the Welsh English Dialect in Fiction. Swansea University.
Lang, G. (2006). Upper Guinea Creole: A Santiago-born language? Journal of Pidgin and Creole Languages, 21(2), 239-281.
